Question: Before May 20, 2024, will it be announced that Minouche Shafik will no longer be President of Columbia University?
llm_reasoning: The strongest evidence as of May 1, 2024 points to continuity rather than an imminent leadership change: Columbia’s trustees had publicly reaffirmed support for Shafik on April 24, and she was still actively exercising presidential authority on May 1 while trying to stabilize campus through finals and commencement. A YES outcome before May 20 would likely require either a very rapid board reversal or an abrupt resignation during an active crisis, both of which are plausible given the extraordinary pressure from protests, donors, lawmakers, and recent Ivy League precedent, but still look like tail-risk paths over a 19-day window. The short horizon matters a lot here: even if her position has been structurally weakened, boards often prefer not to announce a transition in the middle of an emergency unless events deteriorate sharply, so the chance is meaningfully above zero but still clearly below 10.

Question: Will Nvidia outperform the S&P 500 in 2025?
llm_reasoning: Nvidia entered February needing to erase roughly a 13-point relative deficit after January, which by itself pushed the odds below a coin flip, because even a good remaining year might not be enough to overcome the early gap. Still, the business backdrop was unusually strong: as of its November 2024 report, revenue was up 94% year over year and data-center revenue up 112%, Blackwell demand was strong, and major customers were still committing enormous AI infrastructure budgets into 2025. Against that, expectations remained very high, DeepSeek had created a real efficiency-and-demand scare, Blackwell had visible execution noise, and tighter export controls added another plausible headwind. Putting those together, Nvidia still had a very real path to a big rebound and full-year outperformance, but it looked more likely than not to fall short.

The positive evidence is meaningful: SpaceX was publicly aiming for the next flight around May, the revised splashdown/environmental work had progressed, and both Booster 11 and Ship 29 had completed major testing. That combination puts the overall probability below 50 but clearly above 25, with 39 as a reasonable midpoint-weighted estimate.

Question: Will the US unemployment rate in November 2025 be below the rate in November 2024?
llm_reasoning: A 39 probability best fits the evidence available by February 1, 2025: the bar for YES is effectively a November 2025 unemployment rate of 4.1 or lower, and while the labor market entered 2025 in better shape than many late-2024 forecasts implied, the balance of baseline projections still leaned slightly above that threshold. The strongest contemporaneous signals for YES were that unemployment was already 4.1 in December 2024, had held in a tight 4.1-4.2 range for months, jobless claims remained low, and the January 29, 2025 FOMC statement described unemployment as stabilized at a low level, all of which make a sub-4.2 reading in late 2025 quite plausible. But the main structured forecasts that matter most here, including the December 2024 FOMC median and November 2024 SPF, centered on 4.3 in 2025 Q4, and downside labor-market risks from slower growth still looked more salient than a sustained re-tightening back toward 4.0. Since those forecasts were only one tenth above the winning threshold, YES should be materially above 25, but because the modal path still sits just on the wrong side of the cutoff, it should remain below 50.
